topic: Someone finds a wallet containing a large sum of cash
raw_output:
[story 1]

The worn leather of the wallet felt strangely heavy in my palm, the sheer bulk of the cash a physical weight pressing down on my fingers. I tucked it deep into my pocket, the sound of the rustling bills amplified in the sudden silence of the deserted bus stop. Every shadow seemed to shift, every distant siren a potential threat. I kept glancing over my shoulder, my breath catching in my throat with each passing car. Who would want this? Someone was definitely watching. I started walking faster, my steps quick and uneven, the streetlights casting long, distorted shapes that danced just beyond my peripheral vision. I checked my pockets repeatedly, a frantic, almost obsessive ritual, half-expecting to find something else, something that proved I wasn't alone in this unsettling, watchful world.

[story 2]

It was tucked beneath a park bench, the wallet a beacon of unwelcome fortune. When I picked it up, my hands shook so badly I almost dropped it. The stack of hundred-dollar bills felt alien, illicit. I hurried away from the spot, my gaze darting everywhere—at the teenagers laughing nearby, at the dark recesses of the alleyway, even at the reflection in a shop window. My jaw was tight, my knuckles white where I gripped my backpack straps. *They know I have it,* a cold certainty settled in my gut. I kept repeating the mental checklist: check the surroundings, check the exits, check the people. Each mundane sound—a distant dog bark, the squeak of a gate—sent a jolt through me, making me tense, ready to bolt at the slightest perceived movement.

[story 3]

The discovery was accidental, a slip of the hand in a crowded market. When I finally secured the wallet, the sheer volume of money made my stomach clench into a tight knot. I moved through the throng like a ghost, my eyes constantly scanning the faces pressed close to me, cataloging every expression—a flicker of interest here, a moment of blankness there. I couldn't settle. I kept touching the wallet, running my thumb over the edges, a desperate need to confirm its reality. My voice, when I finally spoke to a vendor, was unnaturally high and clipped. "Excuse me," I managed, my eyes fixed on the ground near his feet, "is this… is this yours?" The silence that followed felt loaded, heavy with unspoken judgment, and I waited, rigid, for the inevitable accusation to surface.
